lens.included_activities: U.S. lower-middle-market firms, if any, providing repeat outsourced breeding, raising, husbandry, or production of rabbits or other fur-bearing animals for external animal owners, research programs, processors, or brands.
lens.excluded_activities: Owner-operated farms selling their own animals or pelts, hobby and backyard operations, passive animal or financing vehicles, wild trapping and hunting, unrelated laboratory-animal species, boarding or veterinary services, and one-off animal transactions without recurring operating responsibility.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: The qualifying model would use recurring per-animal, capacity, husbandry, breeding, or production agreements with accountability for animal care, biosecurity, records, and delivery. Public sources document animal and pelt production but do not establish an outsourced-service population or representative contracts inside 112930.
lens.deviation_from_default: Narrowed to repeat outsourced animal production because 112930 combines rabbit markets with mink and other fur-animal production and is primarily product farming, while the fixed lens requires recurring outsourced service. The included end markets have materially different demand and regulatory profiles.

a.bridge: USDA all-farm occupation shares, BLS animal-production context, and ILO broad-occupation GenAI exposure are translated into a wage-weighted current opportunity for 112930. This may overstate exposure because daily husbandry is physical and understate it where breeding records, health monitoring, regulatory documentation, scheduling, and sales carry higher wages.
a.rationale: AI can assist breeding and health records, feed planning, monitoring alerts, compliance documentation, inventory, scheduling, procurement, and customer communication. Feeding, watering, cleaning, handling, observation, breeding, veterinary coordination, and facility maintenance remain physical and continuous.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task study from commercial rabbit and fur-animal operations, including owner labor, automated feeding and monitoring, recordkeeping, animal-care time, and current software use, would materially change the estimate.
caveats: USDA occupation shares cover all agriculture, not this niche.
BLS animal-production data are broader than 112930.
The frozen labor-share input is missing, preventing combination with a measured industry labor intensity.

rho.rationale: Recordkeeping, monitoring triage, feed and breeding planning, compliance support, inventory, and administrative workflows can be implemented within five years. Small operation scale, limited digital systems, animal-welfare accountability, biological variability, and the need for direct observation constrain adoption.
rho.change_evidence: Multi-cycle pilots reporting adoption, animal health and mortality, feed conversion, labor hours, false alerts, record accuracy, compliance outcomes, and system cost would materially change the range.
caveats: No 112930-specific AI implementation study was found.
Implementation differs across fur, meat, pet, breeding, and research rabbit operations.

e.rationale: Both frozen dataset inputs are missing, and public agriculture sources do not distinguish animal-owning product farms from firms supplying a recurring outsourced raising service. Neither the LMM denominator nor eligible numerator can be bounded defensibly.
e.change_evidence: A verified operation-to-firm roster with normalized EBITDA, animal ownership, customer contracts, revenue model, recurring capacity, control eligibility, and NAICS classification is required.
caveats: Census of Agriculture operation counts include small farms and are not LMM firm counts.
Animal inventories do not reveal outsourced-service eligibility or transferable enterprise ownership.

s5.rationale: No defensible eligible LMM service population was identified, so a five-year qualifying-control-transfer probability cannot be distinguished from ordinary farm succession, animal or facility sales, and closures in a declining pelt market.
s5.change_evidence: After establishing eligible firms, principal-owner ages, facility and animal ownership, customer-contract transferability, succession plans, broker mandates, closures, and five-year control transactions would be required.
caveats: Farm or animal sales do not necessarily transfer a recurring customer-service business.
Industry contraction can produce closures or asset liquidation rather than qualifying transfers.

q.rationale: No verified outsourced-service population or representative husbandry contract was found. Pelt prices, live-animal sales, and farm margins do not reveal how a customer and operator would share implemented labor benefits.
q.change_evidence: Verified contract-production agreements showing per-animal or capacity fees, cost reimbursement, feed pass-through, performance terms, renewal cadence, compliance obligations, and customer concentration are required.
caveats: Commodity pelt or animal prices are not service billing structures.
Research, meat, breeding, pet, and fur contracts would likely have very different sharing economics.

d5.bridge: USDA mink-pelt production and Census of Agriculture rabbit and mink inventories are translated into year-five demand for a hypothetical outsourced animal-production basket. This may overstate the lens if declining farms are owner-operated and understate it if specialized research or food rabbit programs outsource recurring capacity.
d5.rationale: The clearest measured segment is structurally contracting: U.S. mink-pelt production fell from 3.741 million in 2014 to 771,200 in 2024, with 2025 breeding females also below 2024. Rabbit markets are less transparent and span live-animal, meat, breeding, pet, fur, and potentially research uses, so they provide uncertain offset rather than a firm growth case.
d5.change_evidence: Separate five-year demand and capacity data for rabbit meat, research and breeding animals, pets, and pelts; mink farm closures and breeding plans; customer procurement forecasts; and verified outsourced capacity contracts would materially change the range.
caveats: Mink is only one part of 112930, while rabbit-market data are sparse.
Animal and pelt production do not directly measure outsourced-service demand.
Small and backyard rabbit operations can distort counts without representing commercial capacity.

o.rationale: Live-animal production requires an accountable operator for daily care, welfare, biosecurity, breeding, facilities, records, and transport. AI and monitoring can reduce administrative and observation time, while customers can self-raise animals or exit fur use entirely.
o.change_evidence: Evidence on customer insourcing, automated feeding and monitoring, synthetic or alternative-fur substitution, research-method substitution, commercial rabbit demand, and changes in legal accountability would materially change the estimate.
caveats: The estimate separates generative AI from conventional barn automation.
Product substitution and market exit belong partly in demand rather than operator displacement.
The accountable outsourced-operator population remains unverified.

narrative.executive_view: 112930 is a small, heterogeneous product-farming category with a sharply contracting mink segment and opaque rabbit end markets. Public evidence does not establish an LMM outsourced husbandry population, while both frozen dataset inputs are missing.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can improve breeding and health records, feed planning, monitoring triage, compliance, inventory, and scheduling. Continuous physical animal care, cleaning, handling, biosecurity, and welfare accountability remain operator-dependent.
narrative.value_capture: Commercial retention cannot be bounded without actual contract-production agreements. Pelt and live-animal prices measure product economics, not contractual sharing of labor productivity.
narrative.firm_availability: Agriculture data identify animals and operations but not a transferable LMM service-firm denominator. Industry contraction may create closures and asset sales rather than qualifying control transfers.
narrative.demand_durability: Mink-pelt production has fallen steeply over the past decade. Rabbit demand may be more diversified across food, breeding, pet, and research uses, but public data are insufficient to establish that it offsets fur-market decline or creates outsourced service demand.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: Key risks are absence of a scalable service target pool, missing labor and firm counts, fur-demand decline, animal-welfare and biosecurity obligations, disease, customer and auction concentration, opaque rabbit markets, small operation scale, product substitution, and nontransferable family farms.
narrative.principal_driver: Recurring physical care requirements and diversified potential rabbit uses that cannot be supplied by software alone.
narrative.principal_weakness: The measured mink segment is contracting sharply, while no defensible outsourced-service firm population or rabbit demand series is available.

sources.title: 2022 NAICS 112930: Fur-Bearing Animal and Rabbit Production
sources.supports: Industry boundary covering establishments raising rabbits and other fur-bearing animals for sale or pelt production.

sources.title: Mink, July 2025
sources.supports: U.S. mink-pelt production, price and value history through 2024, plus 2024 and 2025 females bred.

sources.title: 2022 Census of Agriculture: United States Data
sources.supports: National inventory and sales reporting for live mink, live rabbits, and other animals in 2022 and 2017.

sources.title: Farm Labor
sources.supports: USDA all-farm occupation shares and wages for animal farmworkers, supervisors, managers, and other agricultural labor.

sources.title: Animal Production: NAICS 112
sources.supports: Broader animal-production operating context, physical production scope, establishments, and injury evidence.

sources.title: Generative AI and Jobs: A global analysis of potential effects on job quantity and quality
sources.supports: Broad occupation-level GenAI exposure and the distinction between technical exposure, augmentation, and realized automation.
